title: The Complete Bikepacking Race Training Guide
subtitle: Everything you need to know about ultra-distance bikepacking races — from systems to training to the mental game.
meta_description: A practical guide to choosing, preparing for, and racing ultra-distance bikepacking events: training, systems, fueling, logistics, sleep strategy, and the mental game.

glossary.B2B: Back-to-back long days use a long ride followed by another substantial ride the next day. They teach you to start again while tired.
glossary.bivvy: A minimal overnight sleep setup, usually a bivvy bag or shelter system carried on the bike.
glossary.catch-up preparation: An honestly limited preparation block for a race that is too close for a full build. It prioritizes what fits and states what the rider cannot compress.
glossary.dark-hours riding: Riding before dawn or after dark with race lighting. It provides practical training and race-specific practice.
glossary.energy economy: The system for taking in, carrying, buying, digesting, and replacing energy over multiple days.
glossary.fatigue resistance: The ability to maintain useful power, posture, appetite, and judgment late in a long ride or after several long days.
glossary.free-route race: A race with mandatory controls but no prescribed route between them. Riders choose their roads and route strategy.
glossary.loaded adaptation: Training with the race pack and equipment so the body, bike, and systems adapt to the actual setup.
glossary.micro-double: A weekday aerobic session split into two separate rides, usually morning and evening. Each ride lasts at least 45 minutes.
glossary.RPE: Rate of Perceived Exertion — a 1-10 description of effort. It matters when terrain, fatigue, weather, and load make power less transferable.
glossary.SIM: Simulation block — a consecutive multi-day, fully loaded rehearsal that tests riding, fueling, sleep systems, navigation, and resupply before race day.
glossary.systems: The repeatable practical setup around riding: bike and pack, lighting, navigation, food, sleep, hygiene, repair, and decision rules.
glossary.Tour Divide-class: A very high-demand, multi-week self-supported race profile requiring large daily riding volume, climbing, logistics, and in-race recovery.
glossary.VO2: Short, very hard efforts that maintain the upper limit of aerobic capacity. They are maintenance work in ultra preparation.
glossary.Z2: Zone 2 — an easy, conversational endurance effort that builds aerobic capacity without excessive fatigue.

sections.title: The Race Has a Different Shape

blocks.content:
An ultra-distance bikepacking race is not a long gravel race with extra food. It needs the same aerobic engine after the normal finish line has passed.

Ask what you can repeat tomorrow after a loaded day, a poor meal, rough roads, and less recovery than you choose at home. That is the problem.

Tour Divide, the Colorado Trail Race, the Transcontinental, Badlands, and Atlas Mountain Race use different terrain and formats. They share one fact. Riders who function across days gain an advantage over riders with one exceptional day.
Ultra racing rewards repeatable decisions. The bike must roll, the body must eat, and the brain must make sound decisions after the novelty ends.

A single-day event centers on sustainable output for a defined number of hours. Output still matters in an ultra, but the limiter moves. Hands become numb. The neck protests. A saddle issue becomes a skin issue. Food loses appeal. A navigation error costs an hour. A morning pace becomes expensive after dark.

Fitness alone does not solve this problem. Gear lists alone do not solve it either. The race uses a stack of systems. Each system is manageable alone. They degrade together.

items.label: Engine
items.detail: Build enough aerobic capacity to ride mostly easy across consecutive days.

items.label: Durability
items.detail: Keep position, hands, feet, back, neck, and appetite functional late in the day.

items.label: Energy economy
items.detail: Fuel and replenish before the energy deficit grows.

items.label: Systems
items.detail: Keep navigation, pack, sleep, repair, lighting, and resupply working reliably.

items.label: Judgment
items.detail: Maintain sensible pacing and decisions under fatigue.

sections.title: Self-Supported Means You Own the Friction

blocks.content:
Most major ultras are self-supported. This does not mean romantic isolation. Riders manage food, water, repairs, routing, weather decisions, and the results of poor decisions.

Commercial services can support resupply where race rules allow them. A team car will not arrive with a jacket, wheel, meal, and revised plan. Know what is on the bike. Know how it works. Know the fallback when the preferred plan fails.

items.title: Test each system
items.content: Ask whether you can use the system while tired, cold, hungry, and beside the road. Do not race with a system that fails this test.

items.title: Test each item of gear
items.content: Identify the problem each item solves. Test each item with the loaded bike. Untested gear remains a theory.

blocks.content:
A normal event allows recovery afterward. An ultra makes recovery part of the event. Eat enough at stops. Dry layers before they cause problems. Protect contact points. Take real sleep opportunities. Avoid a needlessly hard climb at the wrong time.

Fatigue still accumulates. Manage it well enough to make tomorrow possible.
The goal is not to feel fresh in an ultra. The goal is to remain operational.

sections.title: Start With a Race You Can Prepare For

ayahuasca.content: With fewer than six available hours most weeks, a Tour Divide-class objective is not an honest preparation target. Build riding consistency first. Choose a shorter route or an adventure without a race clock. Learn the systems without betting the season on a rescue.

finisher.content: Choose a first ultra whose daily demands leave room to learn. Prioritize manageable logistics, a clear resupply pattern, and enough preparation time for long days and systems testing.

competitor.content: Use race selection to choose a demand profile, not only a famous name. Compare terrain, altitude, technicality, route format, and the practical cost of a mistake.

podium.content: Find where the race compounds stress. Make that exact stress routine in preparation without damaging recovery.

blocks.content: Ambition is useful. A calendar still sets limits. Choose a race with enough runway to build, test, recover, and arrive with more than intentions in a spreadsheet.

sources.claim: Ultra bikepacking changes the performance target from a single long effort to repeatability across loaded days.
Durability includes power, posture, appetite, and judgment late in a multi-day effort.
In-race recovery includes sleep systems, refeeding, and tissue management.
Commercial ultra preparation treats comfort systems, logistics, and mental preparation as structured content alongside training.

chapters.title: Choosing Your Race
chapters.subtitle: Use the Ultra Shelf as a Starting Point

sections.title: Choose a Demand Profile, Not a Poster

blocks.content:
Famous races are famous for reasons. Fame does not prescribe training. Choose by the stresses the route repeats: climbing, rough terrain, remoteness, heat or cold, altitude, navigation, resupply spacing, and practical sleep setup.

A shorter route can demand more than a longer route. It may require hike-a-bike, technical descents, high altitude, or difficult resupply. A manageable map can hide costly stops. Read the course as a chain of days, not one large total.
Distance is a clue. The daily cost of moving through the route is the real question.

blocks.caption: Selected Ultra & Bikepacking Races in the Gravel God Database
rows: Long daily repeatability, remoteness, climbing, and systems across a huge route.
Technical mountain riding, altitude, hike-a-bike, and structural durability.
Route choice, navigation, self-supported strategy, and continental-scale logistics.
Heat, rough terrain, climbing, and a compact, punishing multi-day effort.
Remote mountain travel, difficult access, terrain, and logistics.

blocks.content: These scores are database ratings. They do not state that one race is universally harder or more worthwhile. They summarize the current race-data profile. Choose the race whose demands fit the rider's experience, available preparation time, and tolerance for consequence.

A fixed-route race provides a prescribed line. The work remains substantial. Follow it, navigate it, manage it, and respond to its conditions.

A free-route race adds a second event to the riding. Riders choose their line between mandatory controls. They must manage route planning, elevation tradeoffs, road quality, border and service assumptions, and plan changes. The Transcontinental is a clear example. A shorter screen route may be slower, rougher, poorly supplied, or unsafe at night.
Free-route racing does not reward the prettiest line in a route planner. It rewards the line that still works after three days of weather, fatigue, and closed shops.

sections.title: Use This Filter Before You Commit

items.title: Check the training calendar
items.content: Count available weeks, protected weekends, travel time, and time for a loaded simulation block. Choose another race if the calendar cannot support preparation.

items.title: Check the rider and route match
items.content: Match the route to current skills. Do not treat high-altitude technical riding, hike-a-bike, desert heat, and remote surfaces as interchangeable.

items.title: Check the logistics
items.content: Budget for travel, resupply, repairs, lodging or bivvy needs, transport home, and contingencies. Include the costs that determine whether the plan works.

items.title: Define the objective
items.content: Define the desired experience. Choose finishing, self-supported riding, clean route management, a first multi-day race, or competition. Do not use vague ambition.

sections.title: Choose a First Ultra With Room to Learn

blocks.content:
A first ultra need not be the largest declaration of intent. A route with clearer resupply, fewer border and route-choice variables, and manageable duration teaches durable lessons. These lessons include loaded riding, small problem solving, eating with poor appetite, sound sleep decisions, and morning restarts.

Choose the experience that leaves room for judgment. Build from it.

ayahuasca.content: Start with supported overnighters or short self-supported routes. Learn to pack, eat, navigate, and repair before adding a multi-day race clock.

finisher.content: Choose a route where repeatable riding creates the main challenge. Do not add every complication at once.

competitor.content: Use the shelf to select the next stressor to develop: altitude, technicality, navigation, heat, or larger daily volume.

podium.content: Study the race record, current route profile, and logistical failure points. Use the score as an index. Base preparation on daily route analysis.

sources.claim: Tour Divide is listed as Tier 1 with a 94 overall score in the race database.
Colorado Trail Race is listed as Tier 1 with a 90 overall score in the race database.
Transcontinental Race is listed as Tier 1 with a 97 overall score in the race database.
Badlands is listed as Tier 1 with an 89 overall score and Atlas Mountain Race as Tier 2 with a 63 overall score in the race database.
Free-route racing requires riders to plan their route between controls.

chapters.title: Training for Repeatability
chapters.subtitle: Build the Rider Who Can Start Again Tomorrow

blocks.content:
No one trains for a 90-hour week — not even the winners.

That is the first fact of ultra preparation. Recoverable load caps sustainable training volume. A race may require seventy to one hundred riding hours in a week. Elite preparation remains far below that. Every rider races in a volume deficit.

Do not imitate race volume until training breaks you. Build durability, pacing judgment, sleep strategy, fueling habits, and systems. These systems let a finite engine do useful work repeatedly. Riders who make ordinary decisions repeatable close more of the gap than riders who collect the largest training weeks.
Race volume is not a training target. Recoverable load is the training target.

A useful plan assumes a full-time job and ordinary life. Design the hours. Do not depend on willpower. Keep most weekday sessions at 90 minutes or less. Use one optional longer midweek slot. Put the long day on Saturday. Add a Sunday back-to-back at roughly 60-70% of Saturday's duration. Attach strength work to existing ride days.

A representative 13-hour peak week includes a six-hour Saturday, four-hour Sunday, 90-minute Tuesday, one-hour Wednesday, and 30-minute Thursday. Weekday riding does not need to become a second job. Weekends provide volume. Weekdays maintain the engine and habits.

blocks.caption: Working-Athlete Tools That Count as Real Training
rows: Count commute riding 1:1. Use two 45-60 minute legs to create 1.5-2 hours of volume.
Use the race pack from build onward when practical.
Split an aerobic weekday ride of at least 90 minutes into morning and evening rides of at least 45 minutes.
Do not split quality work, long days, back-to-backs, or simulation days.
Use race lighting before dawn or after dark at least once every 14 days from build onward.
Use safe routes or a trainer substitute when conditions are unsafe.
Use the trainer for structured weekday work. Count the actual time.
Ride errands with the race pack when appropriate.
Treat these rides as systems practice, not junk mileage.
Book required work time off when the simulation block needs Friday or Monday.
Protect the simulation block and recovery window.

blocks.content:
Ultra races do not use VO2 effort. VO2 work therefore has a small, specific role: ceiling maintenance. All-day pace sits below aerobic capacity. When the ceiling declines, usable easy pace declines with it.

Keep the dosage modest. Include at least one VO2-containing quality session in each rolling 21-day base and build period. Use four to five three-minute hard efforts with full recovery. Place other quality work closer to race demand. Use controlled tempo or threshold late in a long ride, when fueling, posture, and judgment are active.

VO2 does not lead the plan. Remove it during simulation and taper periods. This is maintenance.
The race does not need a rider who wins a three-minute effort. It needs a rider whose ordinary pace stays below a maintained ceiling.

steps.label: 16-week structure
steps.content: Use this compact full structure when the rider already has suitable base fitness. Progress through base, durability build, race specificity with a two-day simulation, consolidation, and taper.

steps.label: 24-week structure
steps.content: Use this preferred structure when the calendar allows. It provides time for base, progressive loaded long days, a three-day simulation, recovery, and systems corrections.

steps.label: Beyond 24 weeks
steps.content: Maintain a durable base until structured build begins. Use extra calendar time for consistency and systems practice, not endless escalation.

steps.label: Less than 16 weeks
steps.content: Use honest catch-up preparation when appropriate. State the missing work. Keep expectations conservative. Do not present it as a full build.

blocks.content: Calendar length matters less than sequence. Build the chassis. Add durability. Make work race-specific. Complete a loaded simulation block. Recover. Arrive without major systems questions.

Tour Divide-class preparation has an honest minimum: six available training hours per week. Below this level, no timeline delivers credible long-day progression, back-to-backs, systems practice, and a simulation block.

Six hours does not promise comfort or performance. It marks the point where meaningful preparation starts for riders with a consistent base and realistic expectations. When life becomes crowded, reduce midweek volume first. Then reduce standalone intensity. Protect the long day, simulation block, and recovery weeks as long as possible.

Fit the plan to life. Do not pretend life is absent.

ayahuasca.content: Build consistency before you use an ultra calendar. Establish six dependable hours before asking the schedule to support multi-day specificity.

finisher.content: Use repeatable routine as an advantage. Protect weekends. Use commutes when possible. Practice carrying the race pack before long days become truly long.

competitor.content: Use extra hours for durability and systems specificity. Do not make every weekday an intensity session.

podium.content: The recoverable-load cap still applies. Keep added work recoverable. Protect race-specific systems work.

blocks.content:
A back-to-back is not two large days for impressive numbers. It uses a long day followed by a shorter, substantial day. The second day usually lasts 60-70% of the first. It teaches riders to start again with fatigue present.

Limit stacking. Outside a simulation block, do not make three long days in a row standard practice. After a back-to-back, take at least one rest day or an easy spin of one hour or less. Schedule the next quality session afterward. Absorb the work. Do not measure success by what you survive.

sources.claim: Sustainable endurance training volume is capped around 25-30 hours per week, while ultra race weeks can demand 70-100 riding hours.
The method's working-athlete architecture includes weekday rides of 90 minutes or less, commute conversion, micro-doubles, dark-hours riding, utility miles, and PTO-designed simulations.
VO2 work is retained as low-dose ceiling maintenance at least once per rolling 21 days in base and build, not during simulations or taper.
Standard full structures use 16 and 24 weeks, with two simulation days for 16 weeks and three for 24 weeks.
Below six available hours per week, Tour Divide-class preparation cannot be honestly delivered.
Preparation needs runway for base development, systems work, simulation, and recovery.

chapters.title: Systems That Keep You Moving
chapters.subtitle: Make the Bike, Body, and Routine Boringly Reliable

sections.title: A System Is Not a Shopping List

blocks.content:
A bikepacking system consists of items that work together repeatedly. These include the bike, bags, repair kit, light, navigation device, clothing layers, sleep setup, food access, and operating routines.

Find each item. Use it. Store it. Continue riding. Do not turn each stop into a garage-floor project. Give the setup training time if it fails this test. Do not buy another product first.
Use a pack that stays quiet, reachable, balanced, and unremarkable after a long day. An exciting setup often remains unfinished.

Hands, neck, feet, low back, and contact points drive ultra performance. They often turn a manageable ride into a chain of compensations. A fit that feels fast for several hours may fail across repeated long days.

Train position. Use the actual cockpit, saddle, shorts, gloves, shoes, and race pack on long days. Note what worsens after hour five. Change one variable at a time. Retest it. The simulation block exposes assumptions that shorter rides hide.

items.title: Test hands and wrists
items.content: Test grip positions, tape, gloves, control reach, and bag interference. Treat numbness as information. Do not endure it until race day.

items.title: Test neck and back
items.content: Build trunk and posterior-chain resilience. Confirm that the pack and cockpit do not force an unsustainable position.

items.title: Test feet and saddle
items.content: Test shoes, socks, cleat position, saddle, chamois, and hygiene routines on consecutive days. Correct hot spots before they become systems failures.

sections.title: Carry a Repair Plan, Not Just Tools

blocks.content:
Build the repair kit around likely failures and personal competence. A spare tube only matters when you can install it in likely conditions. A chain tool only matters when it fits the drivetrain and you carry a quick link.

Practice routine repairs: flat, plug, tube, chain, loose bolt, brake rub, charging-cable failure, and navigation-device restart. Confirm that the pack stores each tool within reach without unloading the bike.
Redundancy does not mean carrying two of everything. It means identifying ride-ending failures and carrying realistic answers.

Dark-hours riding provides race specificity. It teaches light placement, battery management, route readability, clothing choices, speed judgment, and the difference between a normal road and that road at 3 a.m.

Use race lighting from build onward. Use lit routes, familiar roads, or an indoor trainer when conditions are unsafe. Build competence. Do not manufacture danger.

Apply the same method to navigation. Carry the primary device. Know its battery life. Know how to load and follow the route. Carry a usable backup. A phone in a dry bag with an unreadable screen is not a navigation strategy.

sections.title: Test the Sleep System Without Training Sleep Loss

blocks.content:
Practice deploying, packing, warming up in, and organizing the sleep setup when the race may require it. Learn what gets wet, takes too long, hides in the dark, or must stay accessible before other bags open.

Do not deliberately restrict sleep in training. Sleep-deprivation training is explicitly excluded. A simulation block can include an overnight when bivvy competence matters. Test the system and routine. Sleep as needed. Recover afterward.
Practice sleep strategy. Do not manufacture sleep deprivation.

sources.claim: Structural durability includes hands, neck, feet, contact points, and low back, and comfort systems are treated as core preparation.
The simulation block tests full race systems, including the pack, sleep kit, resupply cadence, and daily schedule.
Dark-hours riding with race lighting is required periodically from build onward, with safe-route or trainer substitutions allowed.
Sleep-system testing is part of preparation, while deliberate sleep restriction is excluded.
Community guidance treats sleep setup and in-race sleeping as learnable practical skills.

chapters.title: Run the Energy Economy
chapters.subtitle: Fuel the Ride, Rebuild the Rider

blocks.content:
Single-day fueling focuses on input. Take in enough carbohydrate each hour, manage fluids, avoid gut trouble, and finish. An ultra adds two layers. Riders must buy or carry enough food through a day. They must replenish after riding and eat when appetite is unreliable.

Ultra fueling is an energy economy. Route, opening hours, town spacing, budget, pack space, weather, fatigue, and food tolerance all matter. Riders who wait for hunger may wait for a signal that fatigue has made unreliable.
Eat by plan, not appetite alone. Appetite is useful until fatigue makes it unreliable.

Use long rides to make fueling automatic. Carry food that tired hands can open. Test food in heat, cold, rain, and easy riding. Know what you can eat while moving and what needs a stop.

From build onward, use a fueling protocol with at least 60 grams of carbohydrate per hour on long rides. Use this as a practice target. Do not ignore individual tolerance. Build a routine that survives boredom and low motivation.

items.title: Store food within reach
items.content: Put the next hour of food where you can reach it without unpacking the bike.

items.title: Use tested food
items.content: Use food you have tested. Do not discover on race day that a drink mix causes stomach trouble after six hours.

items.title: Carry a fallback
items.content: Carry simple, widely available food that works when planned food is unavailable.

sections.title: Refuel for Tomorrow Before You Need Tomorrow

blocks.content:
Daily replenishment uses practical arithmetic. Buy food. Eat enough. Replace fluids and salt as conditions require. Leave each stop with the next segment covered.

Plan likely resupply towns. Do not depend on one shop opening exactly when expected. Carry a buffer. Know what a useful grocery-store stop provides. Eat a substantial meal, then secure food for the next day.
The best resupply stop gets calories into the rider and creates a workable next segment.

sections.title: Train the Gut and the Resupply Plan Together

blocks.content:
A nutrition plan that only works with measured kitchen products is not an ultra plan. Test gas-station food, café food, grocery-store food, packable food, and meal timing around riding.

During a simulation block, rehearse the decision chain. Decide what to buy, carry, eat now, reserve, and replace when preferred food is unavailable. This makes nutrition operational.

ayahuasca.content: Practice regular eating on every ride longer than two hours. Build consistency before complexity.

finisher.content: Use each long ride to test one practical question. Identify food that remains appealing, stays reachable, and works at local resupply stops.

competitor.content: Practice the full sequence on back-to-backs. Fuel during the ride. Replenish after it. Carry next-day food. Start again without waiting for perfect appetite.

podium.content: Treat resupply time and food decisions as pacing variables. Use the plan that preserves movement and judgment while controlling the energy deficit.

blocks.content:
Do not wait for a dramatic bonk. Simplify the decision when food loses appeal. Eat small, frequent portions. Change texture or temperature. Drink calories when tolerated. Stop before the deficit becomes a crisis.

Exact solutions vary by rider and route. Notice the trend early. An ultra gives a modest deficit enough time to become tomorrow's problem.

blocks.question: What is the most useful purpose of nutrition practice during a loaded simulation?

options.text: To prove that the rider can tolerate the most expensive sports nutrition available
To test buying, carrying, eating, and replenishing food under realistic route conditions
To avoid eating real food until race day

blocks.explanation: Ultra nutrition is an energy economy. The practical system matters as much as the hourly target.

sources.claim: Ultra nutrition includes in-ride fueling, daily replenishment, and appetite management.
Long rides from the build phase use a fueling target of at least 60 grams of carbohydrate per hour.
Simulation blocks require fully loaded race systems and test resupply and daily schedule assumptions.
Verified commercial ultra preparation includes nutrition as structured curriculum.

chapters.title: The Mental Game
chapters.subtitle: Borrow Judgment From Your Rested Self

sections.title: The Mind Is a System Too

blocks.content:
Fatigue changes judgment. This is part of the race, not a character flaw. After several days of riding, small problems can appear to prove that the project failed. The brain argues effectively for immediate relief.

Prepare for that version of the rider. Do not aim for permanent cheerfulness. Make fewer major decisions in the worst mental state.
Do not expect tired judgment to become wise through personality. Give it a simple framework before the race.

Create a short pre-commitment document before the race. Define conditions that require stopping, rest and reassessment, or continued riding. Include medical red flags, unsafe weather, unmanageable equipment failures, and personal boundaries.

This document does not require riding through every problem. It gives permission to stop when a real stop condition exists. It also prevents an ordinary low point from becoming a verdict.

items.title: Define stop conditions
items.content: List serious injury symptoms, unsafe exposure, inability to operate safely, and mechanical failures without a realistic repair path.

items.title: Define rest and reassess conditions
items.content: List accumulating fatigue, poor fueling, rising irritability, and navigation spirals. Use food, warmth, sleep, and daylight before deciding.

items.title: Define continue conditions
items.content: Classify expected discomfort, boredom, slow patches, and normal low points. Do not treat them as automatic proof that the race has ended.

blocks.content:
The full route is too large to guide emotion. Break it into the next useful unit: this climb, weather window, town, food stop, sleep decision, or morning.

Segmentation keeps attention on decisions you can make. Riders cannot pedal the remaining continent. They can eat now, refill water, select a safe descent pace, and reach the next resupply.
Make the next good decision. Then make another one.

sections.title: Rehearse Low Points Without Rehearsing Sleep Deprivation

blocks.content:
A simulation block should include ordinary friction that exposes systems. Use accumulated leg fatigue, imperfect weather, unappealing food, a long loaded day, and morning restart friction.

Do not use deliberate sleep restriction in training. Sleep-deprivation training is explicitly disavowed. Practice the sleep system. Practice an overnight when relevant. Practice decision rules for needed rest. Then allow normal sleep and recovery. Rehearse useful conditions. Do not manufacture impairment.
Low-point practice means managing fatigue and friction. It does not mean training on intentionally inadequate sleep.

The opening hours offer many ways to spend tomorrow's legs. Other riders leave. The route feels fresh. Climbs invite proving. Do not force meaning onto the race before it starts.

Use RPE as the primary governor. Power and heart rate provide useful information. A loaded bike, rough terrain, heat, altitude, stops, and fatigue change the meaning of numbers. Use the pace that leaves enough rider for the next day.

ayahuasca.content: Begin easier than necessary. Early patience creates speed later.

finisher.content: Set a pace that allows eating, navigation, and basic conversation. The race adds difficulty without help.

competitor.content: Use early restraint as a competitive choice. A small pace reduction protects systems that create later speed.

podium.content: Treat each hard move as an investment decision. Determine whether it improves the race outcome or only satisfies the urge to establish position.

sources.claim: Verified ultra preparation includes pre-written quitting rules for the rider whose judgment is degraded by fatigue.
Segmented goals and pre-commitment documents are part of the method's mental preparation.
RPE is primary and power or heart rate secondary for ultra intensity guidance.
Deliberate sleep restriction is excluded from preparation.
Commercial ultra coaching includes a Mental Bedrock Document and pre-written quitting rules.

blocks.content:
Route research, gear choices, resupply mapping, sleep-system testing, and transport are preparation work. Do not leave them for the final week. Schedule them as seriously as training rides because they use the same attention.

Use decision deadlines. Stabilize the bike setup by one deadline. Test resupply assumptions by the next. Near the race, add nothing unless a real failure requires it. Do not carry one hundred unresolved questions to the start.
An untested open decision is unfinished work, not flexibility.

Map totals are useful. Daily consequences are more useful. Identify long food and water gaps. Identify climbs after difficult sections. Identify conditions in heat, rain, wind, and darkness. Identify surface changes. Identify the cost of a mechanical in each location.

For fixed routes, build a daily risk map. For free-route events, build route-choice rules. Define when to trade distance for services, avoid a scenic slow line, or preserve options instead of committing to one plan.

items.title: Record services
items.content: Record likely hours, alternatives, water opportunities, cash needs, and the result of a closed service point.

items.title: Mark terrain and weather
items.content: Mark sections where surface, altitude, temperature, wind, or remoteness change speed and safety assumptions.

items.title: Identify decision points
items.content: Identify points where you must reroute, sleep, or resupply before options disappear.

sections.title: Build a Resupply Map With Buffers

blocks.content:
Resupply planning does not predict the exact sandwich at an exact hour. It establishes the route's food and water rhythm, then provides a buffer for imperfect conditions.

Build a simple map or sheet with primary stops, fallback stops, likely closing times, water gaps, and required carry for the next segment. Test it during training. Use a resupply plan that speeds decisions. Do not build a fragile script.
The plan should ease the next decision. A closed convenience store should not become a catastrophe.

Experiment with gear early enough to let it fail. Use build and simulation periods to test pack balance, rain systems, light runtime, charging, sleep setup, repair access, and clothing layers. Keep notes after each long day.

Resolve the findings after the simulation block. Do not add novelty because online gear discussion makes a known setup look old. A known solution has more value than a promising solution at the start line.

ayahuasca.content: Build one simple setup and repeat it. Build fluency. Do not chase maximum bag capacity.

finisher.content: Test one system on each long ride. Record the result before memory changes it.

competitor.content: Use back-to-backs to test whether the setup works on the second morning, not only the first afternoon.

podium.content: Treat every system as a time-and-risk tradeoff. Use the arrangement that limits unnecessary stops and avoids preventable failures.

sections.title: Plan the Beginning and the Exit

blocks.content:
Plan travel, bike transport, start-town food, device charging, registration requirements, insurance, communications, and the route home. Plan the exit too. Define actions for a scratch, major mechanical, illness, or needed help within race rules.

A sound exit plan does not increase the chance of scratching. It makes the race safer and removes pressure to turn every problem into a stubbornness test.

sources.claim: Verified commercial ultra programs package gear, route research, resupply mapping, and sleep-system selection as core preparation.
For free-route races, route design between controls is a competitive discipline.
Preparation includes route research, gear and sleep-system testing, resupply planning, and mental preparation.
Published guidance addresses free-route planning and sleep strategy for ultra races.

chapters.title: Simulation and Race Week
chapters.subtitle: Arrive With Answers, Not New Ideas

sections.title: The Simulation Is a Systems Test

blocks.content:
A simulation block cannot recreate a multi-week race. Do not try. Concentrate useful problems while time remains to solve them. Test loaded consecutive days, fueling under fatigue, sleep setup, morning restart friction, contact points, navigation, resupply timing, and the intended kit.

Produce a list of answers. Record what rubbed, stayed inaccessible, failed as food, used more battery, cost too much pace, or slowed the next morning. That list has more value than a dramatic training story.
A simulation block succeeds when it exposes problems early enough to correct them.

A standard 16-week structure uses a two-day simulation block. A standard 24-week structure uses three days. Longer custom structures can use up to four. Use consecutive, fully loaded days. Anchor them around a weekend when possible.

Use Saturday and Sunday for a two-day block. This needs no PTO. Use Friday through Sunday for a three-day block. This needs one day away from work. Use Friday through Monday for a four-day block. This needs two days. Book the time early. Protect this training block from casual calendar changes.

blocks.caption: Simulation Design Principles
rows: Use the race pack and systems intended for the event.
Use consecutive days that match the preparation structure. Do not use a one-day epic.
Follow the practiced in-ride protocol. Rehearse practical resupply.
Test the sleep system when relevant. Do not deliberately restrict sleep.
Use the actual device setup and backup plan.
Protect the recovery period afterward. Treat absorption as part of the prescription.

blocks.content:
A simulation block creates major training stress. It is not a final exam followed by more proving. Begin the next block with rest or recovery spins. Do not add intensity. Use the recovery window to absorb load and correct exposed systems failures.

Hard training immediately afterward can turn useful information into lingering fatigue. Recovery remains part of the simulation prescription.
Do the work. Learn from it. Recover enough to use what it taught you.

sections.title: Race Week Is for Closing Loops

blocks.content:
Use the final phase for consolidation and taper. Do not search for late fitness. Reduce volume. Maintain familiar movement. Protect sleep, food, travel, and calm. No new bike setup, nutrition theory, route obsession, or heroic long ride will save the race.

Close practical loops. Charge devices. Confirm files and backups. Inspect the bike. Prepare documents. Pack in a repeatable order. Confirm the opening resupply plan. Review decision rules. Then leave the setup alone.

items.title: Check the bike
items.content: Inspect wear items. Test shifting and braking. Confirm tire condition and sealant. Secure all fasteners and mounts.

items.title: Check navigation and power
items.content: Confirm route files, device settings, charging cables, batteries, mounts, and the backup method.

items.title: Prepare food and water
items.content: Prepare the opening segment. Do not carry food for the whole race. Confirm the first meaningful resupply and a buffer.

items.title: Prepare the mind
items.content: Read decision rules. Define the first segment. Leave the remaining race for tomorrow.

sections.title: Start Conservatively Enough to Still Be Racing Later

blocks.content:
The start does not demonstrate fitness. It enters the race without creating debt that takes days to repay. Ride the opening segment at a pace that allows eating, drinking, bike observation, and quiet adjustments.

Use systems exactly as practiced. Follow the next-segment plan. Do not process the whole route at once. Solve the smallest useful problem first: food, water, warmth, light, navigation, sleep, repair, then pace.

ayahuasca.content: Make calm decisions and maintain a sustainable effort. The race does not need an opening statement.

finisher.content: Begin with enough restraint to keep food, navigation, and body checks easy. The route supplies its own difficulty.

competitor.content: Make early pace serve the full race. Do not spend tomorrow's resilience to win an opening-hour comparison.

podium.content: Start with tactical patience. Move only when the race outcome benefits. Do not react because early gaps feel permanent.

sources.claim: Simulation blocks are two days for 16-week structures and three days for 24-week structures, with fully loaded systems and protected placement.
Simulation scheduling uses weekend layouts and defined PTO requirements for two-, three-, and four-day blocks.
The post-simulation recovery period begins with rest or recovery spins and no intensity.
Simulations test practical systems, including gear, sleep, resupply, fueling, navigation, and daily schedule.
Deliberate sleep-deprivation training is excluded even when sleep systems are rehearsed.
